_meta.name: Tidal Secrets Knowledge Base
_meta.description: Complete world knowledge for a medieval coastal fishing village. Five categories covering daily village life, kingdom politics, the imprisoned cartographer, maritime lore, and the hidden truth beneath the arrest.

village_life.description: Daily life in Saltmere, the fishing village — its economy, people, routines, and recent changes

depths.0: Saltmere is a small fishing village on the kingdom's southern coast. Most families earn their living from the sea. The daily rhythm turns on the tides — boats go out before dawn, catch is sold at market by mid-morning, nets are mended in the afternoon. There is one inn, the Salted Anchor, and a small market that grows larger when merchant ships pass through.
depths.1: The Pemwick family and the Strand family have not spoken in eleven years over a dispute about dock rights that was never formally resolved. The innkeeper, a heavyset widow named Bross, knows every rumor in Saltmere within a day of its birth and trades information like a secondary currency. Several fishermen owe Caspian favors going back a decade — he has never called them in, which makes the debt feel larger the longer it sits. The old village elder, Tomas Adrel, died last winter of a fever that came on quickly and left quickly, taking him with it. His death struck many as odd given his robust health the month prior.
depths.2: Elder Adrel's replacement was not chosen by the village in the traditional way. Within three weeks of his death, a man named Caswell arrived from the capital on a horse with a royal warrant naming him the crown's appointed village steward. He smiles readily and asks careful questions about who fishes what waters and what the catch has been each season — questions that sound like friendliness and feel like inventory. Two of the older fishermen have noticed he maps the dock with his eyes. There are whispered comparisons to the tax assessors who came through the region four years ago before the levy was raised.
depths.3: Caspian knows about the sea cave. It sits beneath the cliffs at the north end of the bay, accessible only at low tide through a narrow channel. It has been used by smugglers for at least three generations — his own father showed it to him when he was twelve and told him it was a family secret, not a criminal one. Currently it is not in use, but Caspian has kept it in the back of his mind as an option. He has never told Edda about it. He has never told anyone. The cave has a dry upper shelf large enough to hide a person, or a document, or a small boat.

kingdom_politics.description: The political landscape of King Aldric's kingdom — taxation, expansion, war, and what drives them

depths.0: King Aldric rules from the capital two weeks' travel north. His reign is marked by increasing taxation and conscription. There have been wars — the kingdom has been expanding its borders for the past twelve years. Most villagers know this the way they know winter comes: as a fact that affects them but that they did not choose.
depths.1: The northern expansion has been the center of Aldric's reign for the past seven years. Highland tribes have been displaced from their ancestral territories to make way for crown-granted settlements. The official account calls the highlands 'uninhabited frontier lands claimed by right of the crown.' Soldiers who have served in the north return quieter than they left. Conscription notices in Saltmere now come twice a year instead of once.
depths.2: The northern territories are not simply open land. There are ruins in the highland valleys — old stone structures that predate the current kingdom by centuries, possibly longer. The king's surveyors have been particularly interested in one valley complex that local guides call the Arch grounds. The nature of the king's interest is not clear, but the expansion's route has been shaped around these ruins in ways that cannot be explained by simple land value or agriculture.
depths.3: The royal surveyor who went north with Dorin's expedition documented not just geography but evidence of the ruins being actively maintained by the highland communities. The settlements near the Arch grounds were not simply displaced — the king ordered the survey record altered so that no evidence of their presence would exist in any official document. The plan for the ruins themselves is unknown, but a second expedition is being assembled in the capital. The king needs a clean map before it arrives — a map that shows empty land. That is why Dorin is in a cell.

the_prisoner.description: Who the prisoner in Saltmere's cell is, why he is there, and what he carries

depths.0: There is a prisoner in the village cell. He arrived four months ago under guard with a sealed warrant. The soldier, Sergeant Hollis, tells anyone who asks that the man is a criminal from the capital and not to be spoken to. Most villagers comply. The prisoner is quiet and causes no trouble.
depths.1: The prisoner is clearly educated. He speaks in the measured cadence of someone trained in court language. He has ink-stained hands. One of the market women overheard Hollis refer to him, once, as 'the cartographer' before catching himself. He receives minimal rations and is permitted no visitors, though no one enforces this rule very hard when it comes to the orphan girl.
depths.2: Dorin Vael was the First Cartographer to the Crown — one of the highest technical appointments in royal service. He was arrested after completing a survey of the Northern Reach territories. The official charge, whispered rather than announced, is 'misrepresentation of crown survey data' — a phrase that means nothing to most people and clearly means everything to the king. Hollis received his orders from the palace directly, not from any court or magistrate. There was no trial.
depths.3: Dorin discovered that the Northern Reach highlands were not uninhabited. He mapped the settlements he found there — their roads, their fields, their burial grounds, their names — because that was his job and his commitment to accuracy. He was asked to revise the survey to remove all evidence of human habitation. He refused. He made a copy of the original before his arrest and hid it somewhere outside the capital. He will not say where, regardless of what is offered or threatened. He has told Mira fragments of the story — not all of it, but enough that she understands the shape of the injustice. He believes the copy map is the only thing keeping him alive: as long as the king does not know where it is, Dorin has value as a prisoner.

sea_legends.description: The maritime lore of Saltmere — tides, weather, old stories, and the deep history of this coastline

depths.0: Saltmere fishermen read the sea the way others read a face. Red sky in morning means weather by evening. A still night before a storm means the storm will be severe. The rocks at the northern point of the bay are called the Sisters — three jagged outcroppings that have wrecked seventeen boats over the years. No one fishes past the Sisters after dark.
depths.1: The Long Current is a seasonal deep-water current that runs close to shore in early spring and late autumn. Saltmere fishermen treat it with something close to reverence — it brings the best mackerel runs but can overturn a small boat in minutes if the wind shifts wrong. Caspian navigates it by a set of personal markers he has never written down, passed to him by his father and adjusted over forty years of observation. Several younger fishermen have asked him to teach them. He says he will, and hasn't yet.
depths.2: The Drowned Bell is a local legend — on still nights when the tide is fully out, some fishermen claim to hear a low tone from beneath the water, somewhere off the northern cliffs. Not everyone hears it. Those who do describe it as the sound of a large bell muffled by deep water. The old people of the village call it a bad omen for kings — specifically, they say, it rings when a king is doing something that will cost him the sea's favor. It has been heard three times in living memory. Each time, within a year, something in the kingdom shifted.
depths.3: The monastery that once stood on the northern clifftop was destroyed two generations ago by order of King Aldric's grandfather, King Harwick. The monks had maintained archives going back two centuries, recording land grants, boundary agreements, and the history of communities throughout the region — including the highland territories. Harwick ordered the monastery burned and the monks dispersed. The bell tower fell into the sea during the fire. The records were destroyed. Or most of them were: there are stories among the oldest fishing families of a monk who escaped with a chest of documents and hid it somewhere on the coastline before disappearing. Caspian's grandmother told him this story. He does not know if it is true. He has never looked.

tidal_secrets.description: The hidden truth connecting the prisoner, the map, and the king's plans — what it means and what it risks

depths.0: Something feels wrong about the prisoner's arrest. He is clearly not a violent man. The warrant was sealed and never read aloud. The soldier seems uncomfortable in ways he cannot name. The village steward arrived very shortly after. These things may not be connected, but they happened in sequence and the sequence is unsettling.
depths.1: Sergeant Hollis has been in service for eighteen years and has never received orders that told him simultaneously that a prisoner was dangerous, must not be harmed, and must not be heard. These three instructions are internally contradictory. A dangerous prisoner would typically be moved to a fortress, not left in a village cell with one guard. The prisoner shows no signs of violence and every sign of education. Hollis has concluded, without quite admitting it to himself, that the man's danger is not physical.
depths.2: The arrest warrant for Dorin bypassed the regional magistrate entirely and came directly from the palace chancery under the king's seal. This is legally unusual — crown servants accused of misconduct are supposed to appear before a sworn court. The fact that no court was involved means no record exists of charges being formally filed, no advocate was permitted, and no law constrains what happens to him. He could be kept here indefinitely, or moved and no one outside the palace would know where he went. Someone with legal knowledge would recognize this for what it is: imprisonment without recourse.
depths.3: The map Dorin copied before his arrest documents the existence of highland communities that the king's official records now claim do not exist. If this map reached the other kingdoms — particularly the eastern federation, which has treaty obligations concerning indigenous land rights — it would constitute proof that King Aldric ordered the erasure of living communities from official record and is proceeding with settlement of occupied territory. This is the kind of evidence that starts wars, or ends reigns, or both. The king's second northern expedition is planned for spring. If the settlements are gone by then and no record exists that they were ever there, the map becomes merely an eccentric curiosity. If the map surfaces before the expedition, it becomes something that cannot be ignored. Dorin knows all of this. He is calculating, quietly, whether anyone in this village might be trusted to carry a copy of his testimony north — not the map itself, but a written account of what he found and what was done. He has not asked yet. He is deciding if the asking would put someone in danger they did not choose.
